_meta.purpose: Per-carbonate-mineral thermodynamic + kinetic data with confidence tiers. Consumed by js/20c-chemistry-carbonate-Ksp.ts (Week 2 deliverable) to replace the empirical eq curves in js/32-supersat-carbonate.ts with Ksp-based saturation indices.
_meta.minerals_covered: 14 carbonates currently in data/minerals.json + HMC (high-magnesian calcite, to be added via vugg-add-mineral in Week 2-3)
minerals_deferred_to_phase_2: vaterite (CaCO3 metastable polymorph) — not in current catalog

thermodynamic.A: ΔGf directly measured (calorimetry OR solubility), ≥2 major databases concur within 2 kJ/mol
thermodynamic.B: Measured by one credible source, no conflicting data, in 1-2 databases
thermodynamic.C: Estimated by linear free-energy correlation OR single solubility with significant scatter
thermodynamic.D: No published thermodynamic data; estimated from structural / compositional analogs
thermodynamic.conflict: Two or more databases give significantly different values (>5 kJ/mol disagreement)

kinetic.A: Canonical published rate law, multiple confirmations over decades (e.g. Plummer-Wigley-Parkhurst 1978 for calcite)
kinetic.B: Published rate law from a credible single source, mechanistic basis (e.g. Kim 2023 for dolomite)
kinetic.C: Rate law estimated from family analog (e.g. siderite rate ≈ calcite rate × Fe-redox factor)

Ksp_convention.form: log10(Ksp) at 25°C for the stoichiometric dissolution reaction
Ksp_convention.basic_carbonate_example: Malachite Cu2(CO3)(OH)2 -> 2 Cu2+ + CO3^2- + 2 OH-, log Ksp = -33.16
Ksp_convention.notes: Activities, not concentrations. Davies correction applied at the consumer side (js/20a-chemistry-activity.ts) when ACTIVITY_CORRECTED_SUPERSAT is on.

T_dependence_convention.notes: van't Hoff form. Exact within ~0.1 log units across 0-100C for most carbonates. For wider T range or pressure dependence, full Maier-Kelley extrapolation needed (Phase 2).

sources_legend.Plummer_Busenberg_1982: Plummer & Busenberg (1982) Geochim. Cosmochim. Acta 46:1011-1040. Canonical CaCO3 polymorph solubilities.
sources_legend.Plummer_Wigley_Parkhurst_1978: Plummer, Wigley & Parkhurst (1978) Am. J. Sci. 278:179-216. PWP calcite rate law.
sources_legend.Kim_Sun_2023: Kim, Sun et al. (2023) Science 382:915-920. Dolomite cation-disorder cyclic-omega mechanism.
sources_legend.Benezeth_2009: Benezeth, Dandurand, Harrichoury (2009) Chem. Geol. 265:3-12. Siderite solubility.
sources_legend.Sherman_Barak_2000: Sherman & Barak (2000) Soil Sci. Soc. Am. J. 64:1959-1968. Modern dolomite Ksp determination.
sources_legend.PHREEQC_wateq4f: USGS PHREEQC wateq4f.dat database. Compiled values; primary sources vary per entry.
sources_legend.PHREEQC_llnl: USGS PHREEQC llnl.dat database (Lawrence Livermore). Comprehensive but estimation-heavy at low T.
sources_legend.Robie_Hemingway_1995: Robie & Hemingway (1995) USGS Bull. 2131. Calorimetric standard.
sources_legend.Bischoff_Mackenzie_Bishop_1987: Bischoff, Mackenzie & Bishop (1987) Geochim. Cosmochim. Acta 51:1413-1423. Stabilities of synthetic magnesian calcites in aqueous solution: comparison with biogenic materials. (v146 prep correction: earlier entry listed authors as Bischoff-Bishop-Mackenzie and journal as Am. Mineral. 72:1316-1326 — both wrong per the published record. The one 1987 paper by these authors covers BOTH synthetic stability AND biogenic comparison, was in GCA, not Am. Min.)
sources_legend.Morse_Mackenzie_1990: Morse & Mackenzie (1990) Geochemistry of Sedimentary Carbonates, Elsevier. HMC chapter.
sources_legend.Davis_2000: Davis, Dove & De Yoreo (2000) Science 290:1134-1137. Mg poisoning of calcite step edges.

thermodynamics.notes: Best-measured carbonate. ΔGf agreement across all major databases within 0.5 kJ/mol. Plummer-Busenberg 1982 is the canonical solubility study. Retrograde solubility (-10.5 kJ/mol exothermic dissolution) — less soluble at higher T.

kinetics.notes: PWP 1978 is the canonical calcite rate law and has been the working standard for ~50 years. Reverse rate k4 derived from detailed balance against Ksp. Mg poisoning at high Mg/Ca (Davis 2000) applied as a multiplicative inhibitor (existing sigmoid in 32-supersat-carbonate.ts).

calcite.habit_polymorph_notes: Most stable CaCO3 polymorph at all surface T.

thermodynamics.notes: 1.32 kJ/mol less stable than calcite at 25C — metastable at all surface T. ΔGf well-measured. Same Plummer-Busenberg 1982 solubility study as calcite.

parameters.scaling_basis: Wollast 1990 — aragonite precipitates ~3× faster than calcite at equivalent supersaturation

kinetics.notes: No widely-adopted dedicated rate law. Calcite PWP × 3 is the standard family-analog approach. Less reliable at low Ω.

kinetic_favored_when.narrative: Mg poisoning of calcite step edges + warm T routes precipitation to the aragonite polymorph. Below these thresholds, calcite wins kinetically too.

metastability.notes: Aragonite converts to calcite over geological time (~10^4 to 10^6 yr in nature). Sim collapses this to per-step rate. The 0.02 per-step value is calibrated for sim timescale where one step ≈ 50,000 years (per existing UI tooltip).

thermodynamics.notes: Ksp value is for ORDERED dolomite (logKsp -17.09 matches wateq4f phase 'Dolomite 401' exactly). Disordered dolomite has higher Ksp (~10^-16.5, less stable). The thermodynamic distinction matters: Kim 2023 mechanism is specifically about producing ordered from disordered via cycling. deltaH_diss FLAGGED (NOT changed) 2026-05-30: ours -28 (Sherman-Barak 2000) sits below wateq4f -9.436 kcal = -39.48 and below the first-principles value from this entry's own enthalpies (ΔHf Ca2+ -542.8 + Mg2+ -466.9 + 2*CO3-2 -677.1 - dolomite -2329.9 = -34 kJ/mol). Real spread (-28/-34/-39.5). LEFT UNCHANGED because dolomite is ENGINE-PROMOTED (CARBONATE_KSP_ACTIVE_PER_MINERAL.dolomite=true) — its deltaH_diss drives logKsp(T) in the production nucleation path, so any change shifts seed-42 output and needs a calibration-aware re-tune (vugg-tune-scenario), not a data-only edit. Deferred to a dedicated dolomite-ΔH calibration pass.

parameters.kinetic_inhibition_unless: cyclic Ω modulation (Kim 2023) OR sulfate-reducing biota OR evaporative dewatering

kinetics.notes: Pre-Kim 2023 the 'dolomite problem' (low-T direct precipitation) was unresolved for ~150 years. Kim et al. 2023 demonstrated experimentally that cyclic Ω modulation across the saturation boundary builds cation ordering progressively. The N0=10 parameter is calibrated for sim timescale where each sim cycle stands in for thousands of natural tidal cycles. SCIENCE IS RECENT: this rate law may be refined or replaced by 2027-2030. The rate_law identifier is stable so swapping the implementation later won't require schema changes.

metastability.narrative: Disordered HMC is the kinetic precursor; ordered dolomite is the equilibrium product. Cycling drives HMC → ordered dolomite transition via Kim mechanism.

thermodynamics.notes: Bénézeth 2009 redetermined siderite solubility with modern techniques after decades of literature scatter (old PHREEQC value -10.55 was off by 0.34 log units; our -10.89 matches wateq4f 'Siderite 94' exactly). Reducing conditions required for Fe2+ stability — at high Eh, Fe2+ oxidizes to Fe3+, siderite dissolves and Fe-oxides precipitate. deltaH_diss FLAGGED (NOT changed) 2026-05-30: ours -20 is the exothermic outlier vs wateq4f -2.48 kcal = -10.38, minteq.v4 -16, and the first-principles value from this entry's own enthalpies (ΔHf Fe2+ -89.1 + CO3-2 -677.1 - FeCO3 -752.0 = -14.2 kJ/mol). LEFT UNCHANGED pending verification against the Bénézeth 2009 full text — IF Bénézeth specifically reports ΔH≈-20 it stands as the modern measurement; if -20 is an unverified estimate it should move toward the -10 to -16 cluster. NOT a data-only edit because siderite feeds the SI_siderite STRIP CHIP, so changing it drifts the strip digest baseline (regen + tripwire update required).

parameters.redox_failure_response: dissolution if O2 rises above gate AND total_growth > 5 um

kinetics.notes: Siderite kinetics poorly constrained — Greenberg-Tomson 1992 is the most-cited but ~30 years old. Rate is confounded by Fe2+/Fe3+ redox: any non-trivial dissolved O2 converts Fe2+ to Fe3+, undermining the rate law's assumption of stable Fe2+. The existing engine in 52-engines-carbonate.ts implements the redox failure response (auto-dissolves above O2 threshold).

thermodynamics.notes: Well-measured by both calorimetry (Robie 1995) and solubility (PHREEQC compilation). Mn carbonate analog of siderite; less redox-confounded because Mn2+ is more stable to oxidation than Fe2+ at typical groundwater Eh. INTER-DATABASE SCATTER documented (NOT changed) 2026-05-30: both logKsp and deltaH_diss sit at the low-magnitude/exothermic edge of the published range. logKsp ours -10.39 vs wateq4f 'Rhodochrosite 564' -11.13 vs minteq.v4 -10.58. deltaH_diss ours -12 vs wateq4f -1.43 kcal = -5.98 vs minteq -1.88 vs first-principles from this entry's own enthalpies (ΔHf Mn2+ -220.8 + CO3-2 -677.1 - MnCO3 -889.3 = -8.6 kJ/mol). No single canonical value (genuine literature scatter, unlike the cerussite/witherite/strontianite sign-flips which were clear errors). LEFT UNCHANGED: observer-only (not promoted, no strip chip) so low stakes, and -12/-10.39 are within the published envelope — picking one database over another would be an unjustified preference. Flagged for a future literature-consolidation pass.

kinetics.notes: No dedicated rate law in the literature. Calcite PWP × 0.4 is family-analog estimate, calibrated by similarity to siderite kinetics.

thermodynamics.notes: PHREEQC value -10.0 is widely cited but original source (Hayward 1962) is dated. THERMODDEM gives -10.84, a 0.84 log-unit disagreement → worth flagging in audit. CONFLICT-borderline (5 kJ/mol disagreement is the threshold for conflict tier; this is ~4.8 kJ/mol). Recommend tier 'B' with the conflict note rather than 'conflict' tier.

kinetics.notes: No dedicated rate law. Standard family-analog with calcite as basis.

thermodynamics.notes: Pb-carbonate Ksp well-established (logKsp -13.13 matches wateq4f exactly + is consistent with deltaGf -625.5). Very low solubility — cerussite is highly insoluble, forms readily in supergene oxidation of galena. deltaH_diss CORRECTED 2026-05-30: was -23.0 (exothermic) which was an unverified estimate with the WRONG SIGN. Cerussite dissolution is ENDOTHERMIC: wateq4f +4.86 kcal = +20.33 kJ/mol; minteq.v4 +24.79 kJ/mol; and the first-principles value from THIS entry's own stored enthalpies (ΔHf Pb2+ -1.7 + ΔHf CO3-2 -677.1 - ΔHf PbCO3 -699.1) = +20.3 kJ/mol — the old -23 contradicted our own deltaHf field. Observer-only fix (cerussite is not engine-promoted + has no strip chip), so zero runtime footprint.

Ba-carbonate (logKsp -8.562 matches wateq4f exactly). Above 811C transitions to a different polymorph (γ-BaCO3); stable phase below. deltaH_diss CORRECTED 2026-05-30: was -15.0 (exothermic) — wrong sign + an order of magnitude off. Witherite dissolution is mildly ENDOTHERMIC near thermoneutral: wateq4f +0.703 kcal = +2.94 kJ/mol; minteq.v4 +4.0 kJ/mol. The -15 was an unverified estimate (one of an identical -15 cluster with strontianite/smithsonite — a placeholder tell). Observer-only fix (not engine-promoted, no strip chip), zero runtime footprint.

kinetics.notes: No dedicated rate law in the literature. Family-analog estimate.

thermodynamics.notes: Sr-carbonate (logKsp -9.271 matches wateq4f exactly). Analogous to witherite; aragonite-structured at low T (orthorhombic). Less soluble than witherite. deltaH_diss CORRECTED 2026-05-30: was -15.0 — ~9x too exothermic (part of the same placeholder -15 cluster as witherite/smithsonite). Strontianite dissolution is near thermoneutral: wateq4f -0.4 kcal = -1.67 kJ/mol; minteq.v4 0.0 kJ/mol; first-principles from this entry's own enthalpies ≈ -3 kJ/mol. Observer-only fix (not engine-promoted, no strip chip), zero runtime footprint.

Cu-basic carbonate. Dissolution involves both H+ and CO3^2- so saturation calculation needs explicit pH coupling (more so than simple carbonates). Stable supergene phase for copper above pH ~5.5 under oxidizing conditions.

kinetics.notes: No dedicated rate law. Family-analog. Real malachite precipitation is often gel-mediated → no clean rate law fits all conditions.

kinetic_favored_when.narrative: Stable Cu carbonate in oxidizing supergene zones; azurite is metastable cousin that converts to malachite over time.

metastability.notes: Azurite → malachite is the classic Cu-secondary mineral conversion (azurite dehydrates to malachite when pCO2 drops or humidity increases).

thermodynamics.notes: Cu-basic carbonate. Metastable relative to malachite; converts at lower pCO2 or higher humidity. Forms preferentially at higher pCO2 (deeper supergene zones or CO2-rich environments).

kinetic_favored_when.narrative: Azurite favored at elevated pCO2; converts to malachite when pCO2 drops below ~0.01 bar (modern atmospheric ≈ 0.0004 bar).

metastability.notes: Azurite-to-malachite conversion well-documented in museum specimens that have aged in dry air.

thermodynamics.notes: Late-supergene Zn carbonate. ΔGf measured by Schindler 1978 with refinements by Preis & Gamsjäger 2001. Some database disagreement (10^-75 to 10^-76 range) but within tier B. ΔH for dissolution is estimated — no calorimetric data. Tends to form botryoidal crusts in low-temperature oxidizing conditions; UV-fluorescent (blue-white).

kinetics.notes: No published rate data. Family-analog assignment; high uncertainty.

logKsp_fit.notes: Variable composition between Cu and Zn endpoints; thermodynamic data tracks the dominant cation. For sim purposes, treat as malachite × (Cu fraction) + smithsonite × (Zn fraction) ideal mixture.

thermodynamics.notes: No direct thermodynamic data published. (Cu,Zn) mixed carbonate with continuous solid solution between rosasite (Cu-rich) and aurichalcite (Zn-rich). For Phase 1: treat as malachite-smithsonite ideal mixture, flag the mineral as tier-D in narrator footnotes. THIS IS A REAL DATABASE GAP — surface in audit report.

kinetics.notes: No published rate data. Family-analog assignment with very high uncertainty.

logKsp_fit.notes: Zn-dominant analog of rosasite. Approximate as hydrozincite-malachite mixture proportioned by cation ratio.

thermodynamics.notes: No direct thermodynamic data. Variable composition. Continuous solid solution with rosasite on the Zn-rich end. For Phase 1: treat as hydrozincite-dominant mixture. ANOTHER REAL DATABASE GAP — surface in audit report.

logKsp_fit.notes: Mg substitution destabilizes the calcite lattice → Ksp increases ~0.1 log unit per mol-% Mg incorporated. At x=0.05 (5% Mg): logKsp ≈ -8.0. At x=0.30 (30% Mg): logKsp ≈ -5.5 (approaching disordered dolomite stability).

thermodynamics.notes: HMC is a continuum, not a discrete phase. Mg content x is per-crystal state (crystal.mg_content). Ksp scales linearly with mol-% Mg over the valid range. Above x=0.30, structure tilts toward dolomite. Bischoff, Mackenzie & Bishop 1987 (GCA 51:1413-1423) is the canonical synthesis-Mg-calcite solubility reference (it also covers biogenic comparison — one paper, two strands). Morse & Mackenzie 1990 'Geochemistry of Sedimentary Carbonates' (Elsevier Developments in Sedimentology 48) consolidates. NEW MINERAL — to be added to data/minerals.json via vugg-add-mineral in Week 11.

kinetics.notes: Mg poisoning of step edges already implemented as sigmoid in 32-supersat-carbonate.ts. For HMC specifically, the rate law is calcite-PWP with Mg inhibition factor pre-applied — that's geologically correct since HMC growth is by the same surface mechanism as calcite, slowed by the step-edge Mg.

metastability.stable_phase: dolomite (at f_ord > 0.7 + cycling) OR calcite (at low Mg, no cycling)
metastability.narrative: HMC is the kinetic precursor to ordered dolomite. With cyclic Ω modulation (Kim 2023) it transitions to dolomite as f_ord climbs. Without cycling, HMC persists as a metastable Mg-rich calcite intermediate. The reactive_wall scenario produces stable disordered HMC (no cycling drives ordering); the sabkha_dolomitization scenario produces HMC early then transitions to ordered dolomite as cycling accumulates.
